title: Liquidation heatmaps: reading where stops are stacked
_note: EVERGREEN (warm-up bank for ad traffic). No invented numbers/prices/dates.
body:
🗺️ Liquidation heatmaps — reading where the stops are stacked

What it is
A liquidation heatmap (we use CoinGlass) shows where leveraged positions get force-closed if price reaches them. Bright zones = clusters of liquidations sitting at specific levels. Think of them as fuel: price tends to get pulled toward where the most leverage is parked.

Why we care
Those bright clusters are magnets. A large cluster below price is a pool of long liquidations; a cluster above is short liquidations. Price often hunts these levels because closing them out creates a wave of market orders in one direction. If you don't know where the magnet is, you can park your stop right on top of it — and get tagged on the exact wick that was always coming.

How we use it
• We pull the heatmap and mark the heaviest clusters around the setup.
• We assume price can sweep the nearest magnet before doing what it 'should'.
• We place the SL AWAY from the magnet, not inside it — so a routine sweep doesn't take us out of a thesis that's still valid.
• If the only clean invalidation sits right behind a giant cluster, that's often a 'skip', not a forced trade.

The heatmap doesn't tell you direction on its own. It tells you where the pain is — and pain is where leverage goes to die. We cross-check it with funding and structure before anything ships as a #Signal.

#CryptoSchool
